agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA presentation from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a significant change to a core logistics domain concept — the delivery promise — which directly affects customer expectations, SLA enforcement, and operational accountability.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — the projected delivery completion time communicated to the recipient."},{"term":"Delivery Promise","definition":"The commitment made to the customer about when their package will arrive. This is the external-facing representation of the ETA."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"A time range (or date range) within which delivery is expected to occur."},{"term":"SLA (Service Level Agreement)","definition":"The contractual obligation defining acceptable delivery timeframes, often tied to service tiers (standard, express, same-day)."},{"term":"On-Time Delivery Rate","definition":"The percentage of deliveries completed within the promised ETA/window. A key operational KPI."},{"term":"Last Mile","definition":"The final leg of delivery from distribution hub to recipient address."},{"term":"Service Tier","definition":"The level of delivery speed/priority selected by the customer (e.g., standard, express, same-day)."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Every delivery must have a customer-visible ETA communicated at the time of order confirmation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without an ETA, the delivery promise is undefined, making SLA enforcement and customer expectation management impossible.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a confirmed order, WHEN the order confirmation is displayed, THEN a delivery ETA (timestamp or date window) MUST be present."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"The delivery promise granularity must be consistent with the service tier purchased. Express and same-day services inherently promise sub-day precision.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A customer paying for same-day or time-slot delivery has purchased a time-specific promise. Reducing to date-only would violate the contract they paid for.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a same-day or express delivery order, WHEN the ETA is displayed, THEN the ETA MUST include time-of-day precision, not just a date."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"On-time delivery is measured against the ETA granularity communicated to the customer.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Measurement methodology is a business policy decision, though it must be internally consistent.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery completed at 23:59 on the promised date, WHEN on-time status is evaluated, THEN it MUST be marked as on-time under date-only windows."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Changing ETA granularity must not retroactively alter the delivery promise for in-flight orders.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Changing the terms of a promise after it was made violates customer trust and potentially contractual obligations.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN an order placed before the ETA format change with a timestamp-based promise, WHEN the order is tracked after the change, THEN the original timestamp-based ETA MUST still be displayed."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Date-only windows imply end-of-business-day as the delivery deadline, not midnight.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The cutoff time is a business policy, but it must be explicitly defined to avoid ambiguity in SLA measurement.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a date-only ETA of 'Friday', WHEN evaluating on-time delivery, THEN the deadline is the end of the delivery operations window on Friday (e.g., 20:00), not 23:59."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A standard-tier delivery order is placed","when":"The ETA is calculated and displayed to the customer","then":"The ETA is shown as a date-only window (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday') with no timestamp"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A same-day or express delivery order is placed","when":"The ETA is calculated and displayed","then":"The ETA retains time-of-day precision (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30') because the service tier demands it"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An in-flight order was placed before the format change with a timestamp ETA","when":"The customer views their order tracking after the change is deployed","then":"The original timestamp-based ETA is preserved and displayed unchanged"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A standard delivery arrives at 19:00 on the promised date","when":"The system evaluates on-time delivery status","then":"The delivery is marked as on-time"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A date-only ETA is displayed","when":"The customer views the tracking page","then":"No time-of-day information is shown or implied in the UI"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"API consumers (partners, marketplaces) receive delivery ETAs via webhook or API","when":"The format changes from timestamp to date-only","then":"The API response schema remains backward-compatible OR a versioned migration path is provided with advance notice"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Same-day delivery with date-only ETA","expected_behavior":"Same-day service MUST retain time precision. Showing only 'Delivery by Today' is meaningless — the customer paid for a specific time window."},{"scenario":"Cross-timezone deliveries where sender and recipient are in different time zones","expected_behavior":"'Delivery by Friday' must refer to Friday in the RECIPIENT's local timezone, not the sender's or server's timezone."},{"scenario":"Weekend/holiday deliveries","expected_behavior":"If operations don't run on weekends, 'Delivery by Saturday' must not be promised. The system must account for operating days."},{"scenario":"Multi-parcel orders with split shipments","expected_behavior":"Each parcel gets its own date-only ETA. The order-level ETA should reflect the latest parcel date."},{"scenario":"Retry after failed delivery attempt","expected_behavior":"The date-only ETA must be recalculated to a new date reflecting the retry schedule, not the original promise."},{"scenario":"API consumers parsing timestamp fields that now contain date-only values","expected_behavior":"Downstream systems expecting ISO 8601 datetime will break if they receive date-only strings. Must be handled via API versioning or default time padding (e.g., T23:59:59)."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Mixed impact. Positive: artificially inflated on-time delivery rates (wider window = easier to meet) may reduce SLA penalty payouts. Negative: reduced ETA precision may decrease conversion rates for time-sensitive shipments. Express/same-day tier revenue is at risk if customers perceive the platform is less precise than competitors. The change could also mask operational inefficiencies that were previously visible via timestamp precision.","operational":"Positive: reduces pressure on last-mile operations to hit exact time slots, simplifies route optimization, and lowers the computational cost of ETA prediction (date-level predictions are far easier than hour-level). Negative: removes a key operational signal — when ETAs are vague, it becomes harder to identify underperforming routes, drivers, or hubs. On-time delivery KPIs become less meaningful as a management tool.","user":"Significant negative impact on customer experience. Modern logistics customers (especially B2C) expect precise ETAs — Amazon, DoorDash, and others have set the bar at hour or even minute-level precision. Moving to date-only is a step backward. Customers who need to be home for delivery lose the ability to plan their day. This will likely increase 'Where is my package?' (WISMPA) support contacts.","risk":"Medium-high. (1) Competitive risk: customers may switch to carriers offering more precise ETAs. (2) Contractual risk: B2B/marketplace partners may have SLAs requiring timestamp-level ETAs in their integrations. (3) Regulatory risk: some jurisdictions require specific delivery time commitments for certain goods (perishables, medical). (4) Measurement risk: inflated on-time metrics could mask real delivery problems until they become severe.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End customers (recipients)","impact":"Lose ability to plan around delivery, increased uncertainty, likely increase in missed deliveries and redelivery attempts."},{"group":"Customer support teams","impact":"Expected increase in WISMPA contacts as customers seek more precise timing information that is no longer displayed."},{"group":"Operations / dispatch teams","impact":"Reduced time pressure per delivery, but loss of granular performance metrics for route and driver optimization."},{"group":"B2B / marketplace partners","impact":"API contract changes may break integrations. Partners with timestamp-level SLAs will need renegotiation."},{"group":"Product / commercial teams","impact":"On-time delivery KPI will appear to improve (wider window), but this is cosmetic — could lead to poor strategic decisions based on misleading metrics."},{"group":"Finance / SLA management","impact":"Fewer SLA penalties short-term, but risk of customer churn and reduced premium tier adoption."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The proposal is described at a high level without specifying which service tiers are affected, the business motivation for the change (cost reduction? accuracy problems? simplification?), or whether this applies to all customer-facing channels and APIs. Several critical details are ambiguous or unstated, reducing confidence in producing a complete and accurate domain analysis.","high_confidence_areas":["Same-day/express tiers must retain time precision — this is a fundamental domain invariant","In-flight orders must preserve their original ETA format","Date-only windows will inflate on-time delivery metrics","API/integration backward compatibility is a real risk"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether this change applies to all service tiers or only standard","What 'by Friday' means operationally — end of business day? end of day? midnight?","The business motivation driving this change — is it accuracy problems, operational simplification, or something else?","Whether B2B/partner API contracts require timestamp-level precision"],"assumptions":["The change is intended for standard-tier deliveries only, not express/same-day","The system currently persists ETAs at order creation time and can preserve historical format","There are downstream API consumers who parse ETA fields programmatically"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The proposal does not specify which service tiers are affected by the change to date-only ETAs. Applying this to same-day or express tiers would violate the fundamental delivery promise those tiers encode. Applying it only to standard tier is reasonable but must be explicitly stated.","affected_stakeholders":["End customers","Product team","Operations"],"options":["Apply date-only ETAs to standard tier only, retain timestamp precision for express/same-day","Apply date-only ETAs to all tiers (NOT recommended — breaks express/same-day contract)","Introduce a hybrid: date-only for long-haul/multi-day, timestamp for last-mile when delivery is imminent"],"recommendation":"Apply date-only ETAs to standard tier only. Express and same-day tiers must retain time-of-day precision. Consider a hybrid approach where even standard-tier orders switch to timestamp precision once the package is on the last-mile vehicle."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Delivery by Friday' is ambiguous — it does not define the operational cutoff time. Does it mean end of the delivery operations window (e.g., 20:00)? End of calendar day (23:59)? This directly affects SLA measurement and on-time delivery calculations.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Finance/SLA management","Customer support"],"options":["Define 'by Friday' as end of delivery operations window (e.g., 20:00 local time)","Define 'by Friday' as end of calendar day (23:59 local time)","Make the cutoff configurable per region/market"],"recommendation":"Define 'by Friday' as the end of the delivery operations window in the recipient's local timezone. This aligns with operational reality — deliveries don't happen at midnight."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The proposal does not address the business motivation for this change. Is it driven by ETA prediction inaccuracy (timestamps were frequently wrong), operational simplification, or cost reduction? The motivation determines whether the correct solution is less precise ETAs or better prediction models.","affected_stakeholders":["Product team","Engineering","Operations"],"options":["Proceed with date-only if the root cause is prediction inaccuracy that cannot be cost-effectively improved","Invest in better prediction models if the root cause is solvable","Show confidence intervals instead (e.g., 'Delivery between 13:00-16:00 on Friday') as a middle ground"],"recommendation":"Before implementing, clarify the root cause. If timestamp ETAs are frequently inaccurate, a confidence-interval approach ('Delivery between 2-5 PM Friday') may better serve customers than removing time information entirely."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA presentation from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a significant change to a core logistics domain concept — the delivery promise — which directly affects customer expectations, SLA enforcement, and operational accountability.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA","def

content.thinking:
Analyzing the business impact of changing delivery ETAs from exact timestamps to date-only windows.

This is a domain analysis task about logistics/courier ETA display changes. Let me think through this carefully.

The proposal: Change from "Delivery by 14:30 on Friday" to "Delivery by Friday"

This is an ambiguity situation - the spec doesn't clarify several things about why this change is being made, what "by Friday" means exactly, and how it interacts with SLAs.

Let me produce my analysis.
